summary: Device policies for Windows Activity History. The local Microsoft OSPolicy.admx file explicitly documents the same policy key, value names, and enabled/disabled values that the app uses for the full activity-history bundle.

setting.name: Windows Activity History
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can keep track of what you did on the device and can upload some of that activity so it can appear across devices. These policy values decide whether that activity feed can exist, be published, and be uploaded.
setting.why_people_change_it: Privacy-focused users and managed environments disable these values to stop activity publication and roaming. Convenience-focused users may prefer to leave them at the Windows baseline.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Disabling all three policies stops activity publishing and cloud upload. Leaving them not configured keeps Windows in control.
setting.professional_notes: All three values live under HKLM policy scope and use direct 1 or 0 integer values in OSPolicy.admx. The current app writes all three to 0.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows policy does not force the activity feed state.

allowed_values.label: Disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The activity feed is disabled by policy.

allowed_values.label: Enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The activity feed is allowed by policy.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows policy does not force whether user activities can be published.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows policy does not force whether user activities can be uploaded.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes all three values to 0, which matches the privacy-focused disable profile.

windows_defaults.label: Windows policy default

states.rationale: Policy is not configured by default.
Policy is not configured by default.
Policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: The CSP page exposes default value metadata, but this record keeps Windows default and policy-not-configured separate on purpose.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: People who want standard Windows behavior
recommended_profiles.good_when: You do not need to force a privacy policy for activity history.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You explicitly want to stop activity publication and upload.
tradeoffs: Windows keeps normal control over the feature.
No hard privacy restriction is enforced.

recommended_profiles.label: Privacy focused
avoid_for: People who want cross-device activity features
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Timeline-style activity publication and upload turned off by policy.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You rely on Windows activity continuity features across devices.
tradeoffs: Cross-device activity history behavior is reduced or removed.
The policy is clearer and stricter than leaving Windows at default.

evidence.title: Microsoft Privacy Policy CSP
evidence.summary: Official documentation covering EnableActivityFeed, PublishUserActivities, and UploadUserActivities.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft OSPolicy.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file shows all three policies using enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0 under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes all three policy values to 0 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak source record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the existing catalog output.

decision.why: The local Microsoft OSPolicy.admx file explicitly defines EnableActivityFeed, PublishUserActivities, and UploadUserActivities under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0, which matches the app's policy bundle.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="EnableActivityFeed" ... key="Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\System" valueName="EnableActivityFeed"> ... <enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue> <disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>; <policy name="PublishUserActivities" ... valueName="PublishUserActivities"> ... <enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue> <disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>; <policy name="UploadUserActivities" ... valueName="UploadUserActivities"> ... <enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue> <disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>
validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file names the same policy key and all three value names used by the app, and it confirms the shared 1/0 mapping.
